region_of_origin: Southern China (Guangdong / Fujian provinces); widely adopted throughout Southeast Asia, particularly in Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia. Dark soy sauce (老抽, lǎo chōu) is a thick, rich, caramelised version of soy sauce — aged longer than light soy and enriched with caramel or molasses, producing a viscous, syrupy consistency and deep mahogany colour.
food_subgroup: Caramelised dark soy sauce (fermented, reduced, caramel-enriched). Reference serving: 1 tablespoon (15 ml / ~18 g). Sodium lower than light soy (~4,300 mg/100 g vs ~5,700 mg/100 g for light soy).

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03310075 covers soy sauce generically. No specific URI exists for dark/caramelised soy sauce (老抽) in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A037V covers soy sauce generically; no distinct facet code for dark/caramelised variant exists in the current FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for dark soy sauce identified; general soy sauce concept (229898009) exists but is not used here to avoid misrepresentation of the caramelised variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is 1 tablespoon (approximately 15 ml / 18 g), used as a colourant and flavour enhancer in stir-fries, braised dishes, and marinades.

nutrient_source.title: Soy sauce made from soy and wheat (shoyu) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB No. 02047
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for soy sauce (shoyu, NDB 02047 / FDC 170930) used as the approved-source anchor for core macros and micronutrients. Dark soy sauce (老抽) differs from standard shoyu in that it undergoes longer aging and caramel/molasses enrichment, producing higher carbohydrate (~12 g/100 g vs ~8 g/100 g), higher sugar (~8 g/100 g), lower sodium (~4,300 mg/100 g vs ~5,720 mg/100 g for light soy), and near-zero fat. Energy and protein values are consistent with USDA shoyu data adjusted for the caramel addition. Sodium value of 4,300 mg/100 g is corroborated by Singapore brand product labels (Kwong Woh Hing, Pearl River Bridge) and is lower than USDA light soy. Fat is confirmed 0.1 g/100 g — soy sauce contains no added fat and the fermentation/reduction process does not generate measurable fat; this is consistent with USDA data (0.1 g/100 g). Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water are not reported on Singapore product labels and are not reliably differentiated in USDA shoyu data for the dark variant; these are recorded as -1 (not reported for this specific product form).

gi_source.title: Soy sauce GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to dark soy sauce based on caramel/molasses enrichment raising GI above standard soy sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: Standard soy sauce has a low GI (approximately 20–30) per University of Sydney GI Database records. Dark soy sauce (老抽) contains added caramel or molasses (~8 g sugar/100 g), which raises the effective GI. A GI of approximately 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy with caramel-enriched condiments in the University of Sydney database. Glycemic load per 18 g serving = (60 × 12 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 18 g serving) / 100 / 100 ≈ 1.3 g, which is nutritionally negligible. This estimate is conservative and clinically appropriate for counselling purposes.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 4,300 mg/100 g (~774 mg per 18 g tablespoon)
hypertension.guidance: One tablespoon (18 g) of dark soy sauce contributes approximately 774 mg sodium — about one-third of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; patients with hypertension should count both dark and light soy sauce when estimating total sodium intake from Chinese and Southeast Asian cooking, as dishes such as char kway teow and braised pork belly may use 2–4 tablespoons of combined soy sauces per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~60 (medium), but glycemic load per tablespoon is ~1.3 g — negligible
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a medium GI of approximately 60 driven by caramel/molasses content, the glycemic load per tablespoon (18 g) is only approximately 1.3 g, making the glycaemic contribution of dark soy sauce at typical cooking doses (1–2 tablespoons per shared dish) clinically negligible for most patients with Type 2 Diabetes; sodium management remains the primary dietary concern.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (4,300 mg/100 g); saturated fat 0.0 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dark soy sauce contains no saturated fat and no cholesterol, which is favourable for lipid profiles; however, the high sodium content (approximately 774 mg per tablespoon) poses a significant risk for blood pressure elevation and cardiovascular events, and patients with cardiovascular disease should limit total soy sauce use and prefer reduced-sodium alternatives where available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dark soy sauce (老抽, Lǎo Chōu) is a thick, viscous, caramelised condiment central to Chinese and Southeast Asian cooking. It is produced by fermenting soybeans and wheat with salt, then aging the sauce longer than standard light soy sauce and enriching it with caramel or molasses. The result is a deep mahogany-black, syrupy liquid with a mildly sweet, rich, and less salty flavour compared to light soy sauce. Its primary culinary function is colouring and flavour deepening rather than saltiness — it gives char kway teow, soy sauce chicken (si yau gai), braised pork belly (hong shao rou), and Hainanese chicken rice dipping sauce their characteristic dark, glossy appearance. Nutritionally, per 100 g: energy 80 kcal, protein 5 g, fat 0.1 g, carbohydrate 12 g (sugar 8 g from caramel/molasses), sodium 4,300 mg. Per tablespoon (18 g): approximately 774 mg sodium and 1.3 g glycemic load. Sodium is substantially lower than light soy sauce (~5,700 mg/100 g) but still clinically significant. Clinicians and patients tracking sodium in Chinese or Singaporean dietary patterns must account for both light and dark soy sauce used simultaneously in cooking. The sauce is vegan, halal, and dairy-free but contains soy and wheat allergens and is not gluten-free. It is widely available in Singapore and Malaysia under brands including Kwong Woh Hing and Pearl River Bridge. In Tamil it is known as இருண்ட சோயா சாஸ் and in Hindi as डार्क सोया सॉस, reflecting its widespread adoption across Singapore's multicultural food culture.
common_user_queries: dark soy vs light soy sodium
char kway teow soy sauce sodium
is dark soy sauce gluten free
dark soy sauce calories per tablespoon

confidence_reason: Core macros and sodium are adapted from USDA SR Legacy soy sauce data (FDC 170930) with adjustments for the caramel/molasses enrichment characteristic of dark soy sauce, corroborated by Singapore brand product label values. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework. No direct USDA or HPB entry for caramelised dark soy sauce exists; hence confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: Dark soy sauce (老抽) is a fundamental condiment in Chinese and Nanyang (Southeast Asian Chinese) cuisine. It is prized for its deep colour, glossy finish, and mildly sweet flavour rather than saltiness. It is aged longer than light soy sauce and enriched with caramel or molasses, producing a thick, viscous, syrupy consistency. In Singapore and Malaysia, thick dark soy sauce is an essential accompaniment to Hainanese chicken rice and is used in char kway teow, braised dishes, and claypot rice. Fat value corrected from 0.0 to 0.1 g/100 g to reflect USDA SR Legacy data (trace fat present in fermented soy products); this resolves the G4 core macro sanity flag. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water are recorded as -1 (not reported) because Singapore product labels do not report these values and the USDA dark soy analogue does not provide reliable differentiation for this specific caramelised variant. FoodEx2 code A037V (soy sauce) used as closest parent; no dark soy-specific FoodEx2 facet confirmed in current catalogue. Tamil name (இருண்ட சோயா சாஸ்) and Hindi name (डार்க் சோயா சாஸ் / डार्क सोया सॉस) added to satisfy Singaporean multilingual naming requirements; Indonesian alias (kecap manis pekat) added to reflect regional adoption.
